aws_instance.headline: Replacing a host destroys this EC2 instance and builds a new one.
consequences: Anything on instance-store (ephemeral) disks is permanently lost — only EBS volumes survive the swap.
The instance ID changes; monitoring, backup plans, or scripts pinned to the old ID stop matching.
The private IP usually changes and the public IP always changes — an Elastic IP re-attaches only after the new host exists.
The host is offline for the whole destroy-and-recreate window.

aws_ebs_volume.headline: Replacing a volume destroys the disk and creates an empty new one.
consequences: All data on the volume is lost unless the new volume is restored from a snapshot — confirm a recent snapshot exists before approving.
The volume ID changes, and the attachment to its host is torn down and recreated.
The host cannot use the disk between detach and re-attach; filesystems on it must be re-mounted.

aws_db_instance.headline: Replacing a database destroys this RDS instance and creates a new one.
consequences: Data survives only through a snapshot or restore source — without one the new database starts empty.
The database is unavailable for the whole replacement, commonly ten minutes to well over an hour.
Verify application connection settings afterwards — the endpoint address can change with the new instance.

aws_efs_file_system.headline: Replacing a file system destroys every file stored on it.
consequences: All contents are lost unless restored from an AWS Backup recovery point — confirm one exists before approving.
The file system ID and its DNS name change; every mount target is recreated and every client must re-mount.

aws_s3_bucket.headline: Replacing a bucket deletes the bucket and claims the name again.
consequences: A bucket holding objects cannot be destroyed — the apply fails midway unless force-destroy is set, and force-destroy deletes every object first.
Bucket names are global: between delete and re-create the name is briefly claimable by anyone on the internet.
Policies, public-access blocks, versioning, and lifecycle rules re-attach only after the new bucket exists.

aws_security_group.headline: Replacing a security group swaps its identity, not just its rules.
consequences: The group ID changes; anything referencing the old ID outside this repository — scripts, peered accounts, pasted console rules — silently breaks.
Network interfaces move to the new group during the apply; connections allowed only by the old group can drop while membership swaps.
If anything still references the old group at destroy time, the apply can hang until that reference is removed.

aws_lb.headline: Replacing a load balancer changes its DNS name.
consequences: The DNS name is regenerated — anything pointing at the old name (records managed elsewhere, on-premises config, hardcoded URLs) breaks until repointed.
In-flight connections drop when the old load balancer is destroyed; listeners and certificates re-attach to the new one.
Health checks start from zero, so targets serve only after they pass again.

aws_lb_target_group.headline: Replacing a target group deregisters every target.
consequences: All targets drop out and must re-register; health checks restart from scratch, so traffic to them can blip.
The ARN changes; listener rules in this repository re-point during the same apply, but anything external holding the old ARN breaks.

aws_efs_mount_target.headline: Replacing a mount target briefly removes the file system's door in that subnet.
consequences: Clients in the affected availability zone lose the mount until the new target is up — active NFS sessions stall or error.
The mount target's IP address changes; anything mounting by IP instead of DNS must be updated.

aws_eip.headline: Replacing an Elastic IP releases the public address itself.
consequences: The old public IP returns to the AWS pool and may be assigned to another customer — DNS records, firewall allowlists, and partner configurations pointing at it break.
A released address usually cannot be recovered; treat this as losing the number permanently.
